result:
At 3:17 in the morning, the city belongs to maintenance workers, foxes, and people who cannot sleep.

Daylight cities are full of declarations: signs, uniforms, traffic arrows, little social contracts made visible in paint and glass. But at 3:17, the declarations dim. The bakery’s bright menu board goes dark; the bank’s polished floor reflects only the red blink of an ATM. A bus shelter ad for a summer perfume glows over an empty bench where no one is trying to become a better version of themselves. The city stops announcing what it is for, and in that pause, it becomes something else—less efficient, more honest.

I used to think insomnia was just a malfunction, a refusal of the body to perform one of its oldest and most necessary tasks. Now I’m less sure. Sometimes it feels like an invitation, though not always a welcome one. You wake at an hour when almost no one can demand anything of you, and for a brief stretch, your life ceases to be communal. The inbox cannot realistically expect an answer. The group chat has gone still. No one is asking where you are or what you’re doing or whether you remembered to submit the form.

At 3:17, there is no form.

There is only the refrigerator hum and your own pulse in your ears and the strange fluorescent clarity of your mind deciding now, now is when we will review every conversation from 2011 to the present.

Insomnia has genres. There is anxious insomnia, where thoughts arrive in stamped envelopes marked URGENT, each containing a different catastrophe. There is practical insomnia, where your brain suddenly discovers an exquisite interest in taxes, keyboard layouts, and whether your passport expires in less than six months. There is existential insomnia, where you stare at the ceiling and experience a level of philosophical curiosity usually reserved for dead poets and undergraduate dorm rooms. There is also the most bewildering genre: creative insomnia, where your mind offers you your best sentence at an hour when your body is least able to write it down.

Some of humanity’s most romantic myths depend on this last kind. We adore stories of artists seized by midnight revelation, of scientists waking to solve a stubborn problem, of songs recorded in one haunted take at dawn. We talk less about the practical sequel: somebody still had to function the next day. Somebody still had to buy groceries, answer emails, and remember to smile during a meeting while their soul left the room.

The mythology of sleeplessness glamorizes the spark and omits the cost.

Still, there is something undeniably magical about the hour when the world’s noise floor drops. During the day, thoughts must push through traffic. At night, they echo. You hear ideas not because they are better, necessarily, but because there is less competition. The mind is a crowded apartment building; insomnia is what happens when the neighbors finally stop running appliances and you realize there is a violin somewhere on the fourth floor.

I used to walk when I couldn’t sleep. Not far, just loops through nearby blocks where familiar storefronts turned unfamiliar under sodium lamps. A laundromat looks different at noon and at night. At noon it is utility; at night it is theater. Rows of bright spinning circles, each with its own tiny weather system. Shirts and blankets tumble in private storms behind glass. There is always one person inside, sitting with the posture of someone who has accepted time rather than trying to conquer it. Midnight laundromat people are philosophers by necessity.

A twenty-four-hour diner can be, too. Diners at night have no main character. In movies, everyone in a diner at 2 a.m. is either fleeing something, confessing something, or solving a crime. In real life, most people are just hungry and temporarily untethered from schedule. A nurse on break. A pair of students sharing fries in silence. A man in paint-stained pants stirring coffee with intense concentration. A woman reading a paperback with one hand while eating pie with the other. Neon turns ordinary fatigue into an aesthetic, and for an hour, no one minds.

The waitress in the diner near my old apartment wore the same silver watch every shift. I once asked her if she liked the night schedule. She shrugged and said, “Night people are simpler. Day people need performance. Night people just need ketchup.”

There are entire professions that know this truth intimately. Bakers begin before dawn, building morning from flour while the rest of us dream. Sanitation workers choreograph an unseen ballet of hydraulics and routes so that, by breakfast, yesterday’s life has vanished from the curb. Cargo pilots cross dark oceans while passengers in daytime airports complain about lines. Hospital staff hand off life-and-death decisions under fluorescent lights that flatten the concept of time. Security guards become experts at noticing tiny changes in repetitive landscapes. Coders deploy updates at 2 a.m. because fewer people are online to be harmed by mistakes. Radio hosts murmur into microphones for listeners they will never meet: truckers, insomniacs, new parents, gamblers, students, the lonely, the overcaffeinated, and the quietly hopeful.

Night is not empty. It is merely under-advertised.

If you stay awake long enough, you begin to perceive the city in layers. First layer: silence. Second layer: machinery. Ventilation systems, distant highway hiss, the periodic growl of a truck downshifting, a helicopter writing invisible circles overhead. Third layer: creatures. Dogs barking three blocks away in relay; birds that got confused by light; the soft rustle of something small deciding whether you are dangerous. Fourth layer: people. Laughter from a balcony, a door closing too hard, footsteps with urgency, footsteps without. Fifth layer: your own interpretation of all of it, which says more about you than about the sounds themselves.

At this hour, memory behaves differently. In daylight, memory tends to file itself under usefulness. You remember where you parked, your colleague’s name, your grocery list, your dentist appointment. At night, memory becomes atmospheric. You remember the smell of your grandmother’s hallway in August, the exact shade of a jacket you wore the day your heart was broken, the pattern of sunlight on a school desk in a room you haven’t seen in twenty years. Why does the mind retrieve these? Perhaps because darkness encourages archeology. Without external input, we excavate.

I think that is why unfinished things feel loud at night. Unsent messages. Unasked questions. Projects paused at 80 percent. Apologies drafted but never delivered. The day gives us plausible deniability: I was busy, I forgot, I’ll do it tomorrow. Night removes those buffers. You are not busy at 3:17. You are simply present with what remains undone.

This can be brutal. It can also be clarifying.

A friend once told me that insomnia forced her to admit she didn’t actually hate her job—she hated pretending not to care about the work she wanted to do instead. During the day, she could maintain the performance of vague dissatisfaction. At night, her mind kept returning to the same image: her own hands teaching children to draw. After months of sleepless bargaining, she enrolled in an evening education program. She now teaches elementary art and sleeps, in her words, “like a retired cat.”

Not every sleepless realization leads to a life change, of course. Sometimes the realization is simply that you need better curtains, less caffeine, and to stop reading geopolitical analysis on your phone in bed. Wisdom comes in grand and unglamorous forms.

The phone, unfortunately, is both companion and saboteur of modern insomnia. It offers infinite distraction in exactly the moments when your nervous system needs gentleness and boredom. A glowing rectangle at arm’s length can collapse the whole planet into your bedroom: wars, memes, recipes, scandals, old classmates, new products, weather maps, strangers arguing about fonts. You can begin by checking the time and emerge forty-seven minutes later with a strong opinion about 18th-century ship design. The mind loves novelty; sleep requires surrender. Algorithms are engineered for the former, not the latter.

I have no heroic digital discipline to report. I have watched videos of people restoring rusty knives at 2:41 a.m. with sincere fascination. I have researched whether octopuses dream. I have read restaurant reviews for cities I do not live in. But on better nights, I put the phone in another room and rediscover analog insomnia: paper book, dim lamp, water glass, stubborn heartbeat gradually accepting truce.

Reading at night is unlike reading by day. Day reading is acquisitive; night reading is absorptive. During the day, I underline sentences to remember them. At night, sentences underline me. A line from a novel can feel like a hand on your shoulder in the dark: not fixing anything, just confirming you are not the only one who has ever felt this way.

That may be the most underrated function of art—to keep us company at in-between hours. Songs for driving nowhere. Poems for waiting rooms. Paintings that remind us someone else also noticed how light falls on a wall at 6 p.m. on a winter day. Stories where characters make avoidable mistakes so we feel less singular in making our own. We often ask art to inspire us, challenge us, entertain us. We should also thank it for keeping vigil.

There is a particular moment before dawn when the sky is still dark but no longer absolute. Black dilutes into charcoal. Shapes recover edges. The birds begin with one uncertain chirp, then another, as if sound itself is clearing its throat. This is the hour when insomnia changes character. In the deep night, wakefulness can feel like exile. Near dawn, it feels temporary. Morning is a machine already warming up.

And then, of course, day arrives with its demands. Alarms. Showers. Transit delays. Coffee. The social choreography resumes. You place your night thoughts in your pocket and go participate in the collective project of getting through Tuesday. Sometimes the thoughts survive the day and prove useful. Sometimes they evaporate by 10:15 a.m., replaced by calendar invites and the urgent mystery of where you left your keys.

Either way, the night has done something. Even bad nights leave residue—a sharper sense of what hurts, what matters, what can no longer be postponed. Sleep deprivation is not enlightenment, and chronic insomnia is a serious health issue, not a poetic lifestyle. But wakeful nights can expose truths that busy days conceal.

If the city at 3:17 has taught me anything, it is that maintenance is sacred. Not glamorous, not viral, not often praised—just sacred. Someone is always cleaning, repairing, restocking, rebooting, tending, checking, recalibrating. This is true of streets and servers and subway lines; it is also true of people.

We are maintenance creatures.

We maintain friendships with brief messages sent at lunch.
We maintain homes by washing the same dishes again and again.
We maintain bodies with walks, water, medicine, vegetables, stretches we keep forgetting to do.
We maintain hope through tiny repetitive acts that do not feel like heroism in the moment.
We maintain identity by returning to what we love even when we are tired.
We maintain love itself through ordinary reliability.

Night shifts make this visible because maintenance is what happens when the show is over and the audience has gone home. The stage lights cool. Tape marks are replaced. Costumes are mended. Floors are swept. Tomorrow’s performance depends on tonight’s invisible labor.

So maybe insomnia, on its better nights, is a maintenance shift for the soul. Not productive in the résumé sense, not efficient, certainly not optimized—but sometimes necessary. You sit with your own machinery. You listen for strange sounds. You tighten what is loose. You notice what has been overheating. You discard what no longer serves. You forgive old errors enough to keep operating.

And if you can’t fix anything at 3:17, you can at least witness it honestly.

Outside, somewhere, a delivery truck sighs to a stop.
A hallway light clicks off.
A bakery oven opens like a small sunrise.
A nurse writes one more note in careful handwriting.
A student closes a textbook and stares at the ceiling.
A security guard pours coffee from a dented thermos.
A fox slips through a hedge with perfect confidence.
A radio host says, to no one and to everyone, “If you’re still awake, you’re not alone.”

By 6:42, commuters will flood platforms, coffee shops will hiss with steam, inboxes will repopulate like fast-growing moss, and the city will once again explain itself in bright, practical terms. But for a few hours each night, it becomes a different place—quieter, stranger, less performative, occasionally tender. A place where unfinished thoughts can surface. A place where maintenance is visible. A place where loneliness and solidarity coexist in the same window light.

If you find yourself awake there, between declaration and daylight, maybe be gentle with yourself. Drink some water. Write down one thought worth keeping. Let the useless thoughts drift by without hiring them as permanent staff. Open a window if the air is stale. Read one page. Stretch your shoulders. Remember that bodies are ancient and sometimes imperfectly timed. Remember that millions of people are also awake for reasons noble, painful, practical, and mysterious.

Remember that morning is coming, even when you do not yet believe it.

And when it does, and you have slept too little, and the day feels louder than your nervous system prefers, try this: move slowly for ten minutes on purpose. Toast bread. Stand in sunlight. Wash one cup. Answer one message. Do the next kind thing. Maintenance.

By tonight, the city will dim again. The fox will return. The diner coffee will burn gently on a warmer. Someone will mop a lobby no one notices. Someone will laugh too loudly on a balcony. Someone will cry quietly in a kitchen. Someone will write the sentence they have been carrying all week. Someone will finally sleep.

At 3:17, the city belongs to maintenance workers, foxes, and people who cannot sleep.

If you are one of them, welcome.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
